---
title: "SportStats — data exploration"
format: html
jupyter: python3
---

Your statistics playground. Render in RStudio (needs Quarto) or run chunks interactively.

```{python}
import sys, sqlite3
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

ROOT = Path.cwd().parent if Path.cwd().name == "notebooks" else Path.cwd()
sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT))

df = pd.read_csv(ROOT / "data" / "matches.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
df.groupby("league_name").size().sort_values(ascending=False)
```

## Biggest wins stored so far

```{python}
played = df[df.completed == True].copy()
played["margin"] = (pd.to_numeric(played.home_score) - pd.to_numeric(played.away_score)).abs()
cols = ["date", "league_name", "home_team", "home_score", "away_score", "away_team"]
played.sort_values("margin", ascending=False)[cols].head(10)
```

## Standings, computed by your own module

```{python}
from src.soccer.standings import league_table
league_table(df[df.league == "fifa.world"]).head(10)
```

## SQL practice (after `python -m src.common.db`)

```{python}
con = sqlite3.connect(ROOT / "data" / "sports.db")
pd.read_sql("""
    SELECT league_name,
           COUNT(*)                            AS matches,
           AVG(home_score + away_score)        AS avg_goals
    FROM matches
    WHERE completed = 1
    GROUP BY league_name
    ORDER BY avg_goals DESC
""", con)
```

Ideas to try next: home advantage by league (t-test), goals-per-match distributions (Poisson fit?), Elo ratings over time.
